title: Dyeing with coreopsis
subtitle: Using dried or fresh Coreopsis tinctoria flowers to dye alum-mordanted wool a warm orange-yellow
excerpt: Coreopsis tinctoria, dyer's coreopsis, is one of the most reliable plant dyes available from a home garden, giving a warm orange-yellow on alum-mordanted wool. Iron modifier shifts the colour to a deep terracotta-brown. Dried flowers dye at half the weight of fresh. This tutorial covers the full method from mordanting through to disposal of the spent bath.
sourceNotes:
Hummel, J. J. The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics. London: Cassell, 1885. Public domain. Internet Archive. General mordant-and-plant-dye chemistry baseline.

Iron and copper modifier colour ranges for coreopsis are observation-led: the base chemistry is consistent with flavonoid mordant response documented in Hummel, but the specific terracotta and brownish-orange shift descriptions reflect gardener and guild records rather than a single citable text. sourceType set to SYNTHESISED accordingly.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flowering plant cultivated or harvested specifically for the pigment in its petals, heads, or whole flowers. Dye flowers include coreopsis, weld, marigold, and chamomile. Yield depends on species, growing conditions, and the ratio of plant matter to fibre.

A chemical added after the main dye-bath to shift the colour of the mordanted fibre. Common modifiers are iron sulphate (saddens and darkens), copper sulphate (shifts toward green or brown), and ammonia or cream of tartar (brightens). Modifiers are used after the base colour has developed, typically in the final 10 to 15 minutes of the dye-bath.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots are dedicated and never return to food use. Wear gloves, an apron, and closed-toe shoes. Mordant work goes outdoors or with extraction.

content.text: Coreopsis tinctoria, dyer's coreopsis, is one of the most dependable

available from a home garden, consistently giving a warm orange-yellow on alum-mordanted wool without the variability that affects many other plant dyes. The pigments are flavonoid compounds that are stable in the dye-bath at up to 90 degrees C. Dried flowers are used at 50% weight of fibre (WOF); fresh flowers at 100% WOF. The iron and copper

give reliable shifts: iron moves the orange-yellow to a deep terracotta-brown; copper shifts it to a brownish-orange. All three results are specific, earthy tones with good depth relative to other home-garden dyes.

items.name: Wool yarn or fleece, pre-wetted in cool water for 30 minutes
Dried Coreopsis tinctoria flowers (50% WOF) or fresh flowers (100% WOF)
Iron sulphate or copper sulphate modifier, optional (2% WOF each)
Stainless steel dye-pot, dedicated to dyeing
Fine mesh sieve or muslin cloth for straining

content.text: Dissolve 25 g of alum potash in 200 ml of hot water in the mordant jar, then stir into the dye-pot filled with enough warm water to allow the wool to move freely. Lower the pre-wetted wool into the mordant bath. Bring slowly to 80 degrees C over 30 minutes and hold for 45 minutes. Turn or stir gently every 10 minutes. Remove the wool, squeeze without wringing, and set aside without rinsing.

Place the dried or fresh coreopsis flowers in the dye-pot with enough water to cover generously. Bring to 90 degrees C and hold for 45 to 60 minutes. The bath will turn a deep orange. Strain through a fine mesh sieve, pressing the spent flowers firmly to recover all pigment. Discard the spent flowers and return the bright orange dye bath to the pot.

Lower the mordanted wool into the dye-bath. Bring to 90 degrees C over 20 minutes and hold for 60 minutes. Turn the wool every 10 to 15 minutes. The colour take-up on alum wool is strong: a concentrated bath at the full 50% WOF dried flowers gives a warm orange-yellow. A thinner bath (fewer flowers or lighter ratio) gives a softer old-gold.

For a terracotta-brown shift, dissolve 2 g of iron sulphate in 200 ml of water and add to the dye-bath in the final 10 minutes. For a brownish-orange shift, use 2 g of copper sulphate instead. Do not combine iron and copper in the same bath. Remove the wool promptly once the colour has shifted to the desired depth.

Lift the wool, cool slightly, and rinse in water at a similar temperature to avoid felting. Rinse until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing and dry in shade away from direct sun. Coreopsis-dyed wool mordanted with alum has good wash-fastness relative to other flower dyes.

Alum mordant (25% WOF) at 50% dried flowers gives the characteristic warm orange-yellow for which coreopsis is known. This is the most predictable result and the one most often cited in gardening and guild records. Iron modifier shifts this to a deep terracotta-brown: earthy and noticeably darker. Copper modifier gives a brownish-orange: warmer than the iron result, closer to a spiced amber.

Cream of tartar added to the alum mordant bath (6% WOF) brightens the orange-yellow slightly and keeps the wool fibres supple. A higher flower ratio (75% WOF dried) deepens the colour further without changing the underlying hue. On silk or alpaca with the same alum mordant, the colour is clearer and cooler than on wool.

items.problem: The colour is pale gold rather than orange-yellow
items.cause: Flower ratio too low, or fresh flowers used at the dried-flower weight
items.fix: Fresh coreopsis flowers contain more water and must be used at double the weight of dried flowers (100% WOF versus 50% WOF). Check which form you have before weighing.

items.problem: The iron modifier produced a near-black rather than terracotta-brown
items.cause: Too much iron sulphate used, or wool left in the iron bath too long
items.fix: Use no more than 2% WOF iron sulphate and remove the wool within 10 minutes of adding the modifier. Iron saddening is rapid on coreopsis-dyed wool; err on the side of less and shorter.

items.problem: Colour is uneven, darker at the fold lines
items.cause: Wool was not turned frequently enough and lay compressed at the bottom of the pot
items.fix: Turn the wool every 10 minutes and ensure it can move freely in the bath. A pot that is too small forces the wool to compact, causing uneven dye penetration.

content.text: An alum and coreopsis dye-bath is low-hazard at home quantities. Once cooled, it can go down a foul drain or be composted. If an iron or copper modifier was used, the spent bath must go to a foul drain only: not a storm drain, stream, soak-away, or compost heap. Iron and copper compounds, even in small quantities, are toxic to aquatic invertebrates and must not enter any watercourse. For quantities larger than approximately 2 litres of modifier bath, contact your local council waste service to confirm correct disposal.
